name: Database High Latency / Connection Exhaustion
actions: Check active connections: SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'active';
Identify long-running queries: SELECT pid, now() - pg_stat_activity.query_start AS duration, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state != 'idle' ORDER BY duration DESC LIMIT 20;
Kill blocking queries if necessary: SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE duration > interval '5 minutes' AND state = 'active';
Check autovacuum status: SELECT schemaname, relname, last_autovacuum, last_autoanalyze FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY last_autovacuum DESC;
Review connection pool settings and reduce max_connections if pool exhausted.
Enable autovacuum if disabled: ALTER SYSTEM SET autovacuum = 'on'; SELECT pg_reload_conf();
Consider scaling RDS instance vertically if CPU is sustained above 80%.
Enable slow query log and identify the top 5 slowest queries for optimization.
Escalate to DBA if query plan changes or index corruption suspected.
caution: Do not terminate backend connections in bulk without understanding active transactions — you may cause partial writes.

name: Service CrashLoopBackOff / Pod Restart Storm
actions: Check pod status: kubectl get pods -n <namespace> | grep -E 'CrashLoop|Error|OOMKilled'
Read recent logs: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace> --previous --tail=100
Describe the pod for events: kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>
Check resource limits and current usage: kubectl top pods -n <namespace>
Identify the exit code in pod status — OOMKilled = 137, config error = 1, segfault = 139.
If OOMKilled: increase memory limit in the deployment spec (kubectl edit deployment <name>).
If dependency not found: verify all ConfigMaps, Secrets, and dependent services are running.
Roll back to the previous stable deployment: kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name> -n <namespace>
Verify rollback succeeded: kubectl rollout status deployment/<name> -n <namespace>
After stabilization, review resource requests and limits to prevent recurrence.
caution: Avoid force-deleting pods repeatedly — focus on root cause. Repeated pod cycling degrades etcd.

name: Authentication Service Failure / 503 Errors
actions: Check auth service health endpoint directly (bypass load balancer): curl -s http://<pod-ip>:<port>/health
Verify load balancer target group health in your cloud console.
Check upstream dependencies: Redis, database, and any identity provider (OIDC, LDAP).
Review recent deployments: kubectl rollout history deployment/auth-api
Check for configuration drift — compare current ConfigMap to last known good state.
If pods are in CrashLoopBackOff: follow rb-002 (Kubernetes CrashLoop runbook).
If Redis session cache is suspect: verify connectivity with redis-cli PING.
Temporarily enable debug logging: kubectl set env deployment/auth-api LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG
If upstream identity provider is down: engage their support and communicate ETA to stakeholders.
Roll back deployment if failure started after a recent release.
caution: Do not disable authentication as a temporary workaround — escalate instead.

name: Payment / Webhook Processing Backlog
actions: Check queue depth and age of oldest message in SQS / RabbitMQ / Kafka.
Verify the consumer Lambda/service is running and not throttled.
Check Lambda concurrency limits: aws lambda get-function-concurrency --function-name <name>
Verify the EventSourceMapping is enabled: aws lambda list-event-source-mappings --function-name <name>
Re-enable if disabled: aws lambda update-event-source-mapping --uuid <uuid> --enabled
Increase Lambda concurrency limit if messages are processing slowly.
Monitor the queue drain rate — estimate time to clear at current throughput.
Check for poison-pill messages causing consumer failures (DLQ depth).
Move failed messages from DLQ back to main queue after fixing the consumer.
Notify finance/customer teams of delay — do not silently drop messages.
Verify with payment provider that webhooks are not being retried excessively.
caution: Never delete messages from a payment queue without confirming their state. Financial data loss is irreversible.

name: Memory Pressure / OOMKilled Containers
actions: Confirm OOMKill: kubectl describe pod <name> and look for 'OOMKilled' in last state.
Check current memory usage vs limit: kubectl top pods -n <namespace>
Review recent changes: was a new version deployed? Was a larger model or dataset loaded?
Capture a heap dump if the container supports it (e.g., jmap for JVM, py-spy for Python).
Increase memory limit temporarily to restore service: kubectl edit deployment <name>
Set a proper memory request equal to ~70-80% of the limit to aid scheduler placement.
Check for memory leaks: review gc logs, object counts, or profiler output.
If caused by a model upgrade: coordinate with the ML team to optimize model size or quantize.
Add horizontal scaling if vertical scaling is not sufficient.
Set up memory usage alerts at 75% and 90% thresholds going forward.
caution: Increasing limits is a mitigation, not a fix. Always follow up with memory profiling.

name: Disk Pressure / Storage Full
actions: Identify which volume is full: df -h (on node or container).
Find the largest consumers: du -sh /* | sort -rh | head -20
Check log rotation policy — is log rotation running and working?
For Elasticsearch: clear read-only mode after freeing space: PUT /_all/_settings {"index.blocks.write": null}
Delete old log indices in Elasticsearch: identify indices older than retention policy.
Increase Elasticsearch disk watermark thresholds temporarily if safe: cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.*
Expand the persistent volume if possible (cloud provider volume resize).
Set up ILM (Index Lifecycle Management) policy in Elasticsearch.
Review and reduce log retention to match the original policy.
Add disk usage alert at 75% threshold going forward.
caution: Do not blindly delete files — confirm what each directory contains before removing.

name: DNS Resolution Failures in Kubernetes
actions: Verify CoreDNS pods are running: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
Check CoreDNS logs: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns --tail=100
Test DNS from a pod: kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox --rm -it -- nslookup kubernetes.default
Check CoreDNS CPU and memory — throttling causes intermittent failures.
If CPU throttled: increase CoreDNS resource limits in the CoreDNS deployment.
Scale CoreDNS replicas to match cluster size (rule of thumb: 1 replica per 8 nodes).
Check node-level DNS settings: /etc/resolv.conf on affected nodes.
Enable CoreDNS caching plugin if not already enabled (reduces upstream query load).
Review ndots setting in pod DNS config — high ndots causes excessive search-domain queries.
Restart CoreDNS pods if state is corrupted: kubectl rollout restart deployment/coredns -n kube-system
caution: CoreDNS restart causes a brief DNS blackout — do it during low-traffic window if possible.

name: Message Queue Backlog / Consumer Lag
actions: Measure current queue depth and consumer throughput.
Identify why consumers are slow: check Lambda timeout, concurrency limits, or consumer errors.
Check DLQ (Dead Letter Queue) depth for failed messages.
Temporarily increase consumer concurrency or scale consumer fleet horizontally.
For Lambda: raise reserved concurrency limit and monitor throttle metrics.
For Kafka: add consumer group members or increase partition count.
Fix any consumer errors causing retries (check consumer logs for exception patterns).
Estimate time-to-drain at current throughput and communicate to stakeholders.
After draining: tune prefetch count, batch size, and visibility timeout.
Implement backpressure handling to prevent future backlogs.
caution: Increasing concurrency aggressively can overwhelm downstream services — scale incrementally.

name: Rate Limit / WAF Misconfiguration
actions: Confirm which clients / IPs are being blocked by reviewing gateway access logs.
Check if the new rule affects shared NAT IPs (common for enterprise/B2B customers).
Identify the specific WAF rule or rate limit policy that was recently changed.
If safe to do so: temporarily disable the offending rule or raise the rate limit.
Add affected enterprise customer IPs / IP ranges to an allowlist.
Initiate a change approval request for rollback if immediate rollback is not permitted.
Communicate status to affected enterprise accounts with an ETA.
After fix: implement rate limit policies per-customer-token, not per-IP.
Add integration tests for rate limit policies before future WAF changes.
Review change management process to prevent unreviewed WAF rule deployments.
caution: Do not disable security WAF rules broadly — target only the misconfigured rule. Get security team sign-off.

name: SSL / TLS Certificate Expiry
actions: Confirm certificate expiry: echo | openssl s_client -connect <host>:<port> 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
Identify which certificate is expired (external, internal CA, intermediate, leaf).
For cert-manager: check CertificateRequest and Order status: kubectl get certificaterequests -A
Check cert-manager logs for renewal errors: kubectl logs -n cert-manager -l app=cert-manager
Manually trigger renewal: kubectl delete secret <tls-secret-name> -n <namespace> (cert-manager will recreate).
For Istio internal CA: check istiod certificate: istioctl proxy-config secret <pod>
Rotate the intermediate CA if it has expired — follow your PKI runbook.
Restart affected pods after certificate renewal to pick up new cert from secret.
Fix the alerting pipeline that missed the renewal failure notification.
Set monitoring alerts for certificates expiring within 30, 14, and 7 days.
caution: Certificate rotation requires careful sequencing — rotating the CA before updating trust anchors causes cascading failures.

name: Service Dependency Unavailable
actions: Identify the exact dependency that is unavailable from error logs.
Check if the upstream service is running: kubectl get pods -n <namespace> -l app=<service>
Check if the service was accidentally scaled to zero: kubectl get deployment <name> -n <namespace>
Scale it back up if needed: kubectl scale deployment <name> --replicas=<n> -n <namespace>
Verify network connectivity from dependent service: kubectl exec -it <pod> -- curl <service-url>/health
Check if a scheduled job, autoscaler, or cost-saving script caused the scale-down.
Review service mesh policies — mTLS or network policies may be blocking traffic.
After restoring the dependency, verify downstream services recover automatically.
Add dependency health checks to your deployment readiness probes.
Audit scheduled jobs that modify replica counts.
caution: Before scaling up, confirm the service was not intentionally taken down for maintenance.

name: General Escalation / Unknown Incident
actions: Gather all available information: error messages, logs, metrics, and recent changes.
Identify the blast radius — which users and services are affected.
Check for recent deployments, configuration changes, or infrastructure changes in the last 24 hours.
Engage the on-call engineer for the most affected service.
Open a war-room channel and invite relevant stakeholders.
Set up a bridge call if the incident is customer-facing and severity is high.
Document findings in real time in the incident channel.
Establish a communication cadence — update stakeholders every 15–30 minutes.
Declare a post-mortem and capture timeline after the incident is resolved.
caution: Do not attempt destructive remediation steps without a second engineer reviewing them.
